coredata.dc:title: A facile enzymatic approach for selective detection of γ-aminobutyric acid using corn-derived fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: A facile and cost-effective enzymatic fluorescent method was developed by using eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from corn juice (Zea mays) via the hydrothermal method. The CDs derived from corn juice are termed as CCDs. Herein, we present a novel enzymatic fluorescence sensing approach for the detection of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), a potential inhibitory neurotransmitter. For this purpose, CCDs were functionalized with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) and Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) via 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride/N-Hydroxy-succinimide (EDC/NHS) coupling reaction. After the functionalization of the CCDs, these are termed as CANs. The CANs by utilizing enzyme GABase were successfully employed to detect GABA by fluorescence quenching through electron transfer between enzyme and substrate owing to the formation of a reduced form of NADPH. The proposed method allows detecting GABA in the linear range of 0–90 μM with a detection limit of 6.46 μM. Further, this sensor was also applied in biological samples such as human cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and serum for the determination of GABA, which demonstrated better recovery results of 93.2–101.5% and 96.4–104.6%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neurotransmitter
Gamma-aminobutyric acid
Human cerebrospinal fluid
Detection
Carbon dots
GABase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment of real samples and their preparation
FTIR, XPS and Raman spectral studies of CCDs
UV–Vis, PL and time stability studies of CCDs
Stability and selectivity studies of CCDs and CANs
Lifetime decay studies and plausible PL quenching mechanism
Real sample analysis for GABA determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological studies of CCDs. a) and b) TEM images, inset (b) shows HRTEM images of CCDs, c) Gaussian particle size distribution of CCDs; and d) XRD pattern of CCDs.
FTIR spectra of (i) pure CCDs and (ii) CANs.
XPS spectra of CCDs a) Total survey XPS spectrum of CCDs and high-resolution spectra of b) C1s, c) O1s, and d) N1s.

UV–visible absorption spectra, Time stability, and Excitation-dependent emission spectra. a) (I, II, and III) UV–visible absorption spectra, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of CCDs, inset: under bright-field light (left) and under UV illumination (right) at 365 nm. b) PL time stability studies of CCDs in PBS buffer of pH 7.4 at different time intervals. c) and d) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of CCDs and CANs at varying wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm, respectively.

a) PL emission spectra of CANs by employing GABase in DI water in the presence of different concentrations of GABA (0–90 μM). The concentration of CANs is 0.25 mg mL−1 (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4) and b) linear relationship plot of (F0-F)/F0 vs. concentration of GABA (0–90 μM).

Selectivity of CCDs and CANs after the addition of GABase with other potential neurotransmitters and other aminoacids. All the concentrations of CCDs and other interferants are 0.25 mg mL−1 (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4) and 90 μM, respectively. Inset represents the corresponding PL intensity photographs of all interferants represented by alphabets from a to l.
Plausible mechanism for the detection of GABA using CANs by the addition of GABase.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of CCDs by the hydrothermal method.
Comparison of current and reported analytical methods for GABA detection.
Results for the determination of GABA in spiked human CSF and serum samples.

head.ce:title: A facile enzymatic approach for selective detection of γ-aminobutyric acid using corn-derived fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and cost-effective enzymatic fluorescent method was developed by using eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from corn juice (Zea mays) via the hydrothermal method. The CDs derived from corn juice are termed as CCDs. Herein, we present a novel enzymatic fluorescence sensing approach for the detection of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), a potential inhibitory neurotransmitter. For this purpose, CCDs were functionalized with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) and Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) via 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride/N-Hydroxy-succinimide (EDC/NHS) coupling reaction. After the functionalization of the CCDs, these are termed as CANs. The CANs by utilizing enzyme GABase were successfully employed to detect GABA by fluorescence quenching through electron transfer between enzyme and substrate owing to the formation of a reduced form of NADPH. The proposed method allows detecting GABA in the linear range of 0–90 μM with a detection limit of 6.46 μM. Further, this sensor was also applied in biological samples such as human cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and serum for the determination of GABA, which demonstrated better recovery results of 93.2–101.5% and 96.4–104.6%, respectively.

The present enzymatic approach based on functionalized carbon dots (CANs) have shown better sensitivity and selectivity in the detection of neurotransmitter “GABA”. Further, this method can be employed for the GABA determination in real samples such as human CSF and serum.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) is the principal inhibitory neurotransmitter in the mammalian central nervous system and is involved in various neurological disorders, such as Alzheimer's disease, epilepsy, panic disorder, and anxiety. Based on the zwitterionic nature of GABA (amine and carboxylic acid groups are protonated and deprotonated, respectively), the detection of these compounds is a difficult task [1–3]. GABA as a neurotransmitter has outstanding benefits to humans, including reducing blood pressure, enhancing long-term memory, and improving diabetes by controlling the secretion of insulin. Extensive research is being performed with the goal of large-scale production of GABA in pharmaceuticals and foods, including germinated brown rice, fermented milk products, and anaerobic-incubated tea leaves [4,5]. A variety of methods have been developed for the detection of GABA, with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography representing the gold standards. However, employing derivatizing reagents, such as 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrophenyl-5-alaninamide, o-phthaladehyde, naphthalene-2,3-dicarboxaldehyde, phenyl isothiocyanate, and dabsyl chloride can enhance GABA sensitivity and provide better quantitative detection results [6,7]. HPLC combined with an evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD) can detect GABA without the need for sample pretreatment or derivatization. However, there are some drawbacks to ELSD use, including nonlinearity, poor reproducibility, and limited sensitivity [8]. GABA can also be detected in in vivo using i) microdialysis (for sample collection); ii) implantation of microsensors or microelectrodes (for in situ quantification); and iii) non-invasive techniques, such as positron emission tomography (PET) and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). Major disadvantages of such a process, including poor temporal resolution and injury-related aspects (for microdialysis), and expensive and time-consuming aspects (for HPLC) [9]. Over the past two decades, the emerging trend has been towards utilization of CDs for their crucial advantages, including high solubility, superior chemical stability, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, and optimal biocompatibility [10,11]. Interestingly, the low toxic behavior of CDs has attracted tremendous interest from researchers who have previously relied on semiconductor quantum dots, which consist of heavy metals, such as Zn, Cd, Se, and Pb [12]. Various methods have been established for the synthesis of CDs, including arc discharge, microwave synthesis, hydrothermal reaction, pyrolysis, laser ablation, plasma treatment, electrochemical synthesis, and chemical oxidation [13,14]. Currently, most CDs are synthesized from a variety of eco-friendly natural green sources [15], which have many benefits compared to other nanomaterials, including chemical inertness, superior biocompatibility, and enough fluorescence. These advantages have made CDs the state-of-the-art fluorescent nanoprobes for sensing, bioimaging, photovoltaics, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis [16]. Simultaneously, boronic acids and their derivatives are drawing increased attention in current research for their broad range of applications, including the sensing of diols and anions, biological labeling, therapeutics, and protein manipulation [17]. Recently, a research group detected the neurotransmitter dopamine (DA) by using 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA). They found that combining APBA with DA can induce electron transfer from CDs to DA [18]. In our previous study, optical fiber biosensor based on commercial CdS/ZnSe QDs was developed to detect GABA with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2 nM [19]. On the other hand, enzyme/nanoparticle hybrid systems facilitate biocatalytic features and physical properties of nanoparticles using oxygen, 4-aminophenol, acetylthiocholine, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH), and hydrogen peroxide [20–26]. Label-free detection of GABA was achieved using the piezoelectric immunosensor. However, surface modification of sensor, antibody reactions, and expensiveness are the complex issues affordable for GABA determination [27]. Interestingly, A. Jinnarak group developed a colorimetric method for the detection of GABA using silver nanoparticles. But, the low sensitivity and minimal LOD are the few drawbacks associated with that method [28]. Further, real-time monitoring of GABA was achieved using micro biosensor microarray sensors. Still a few limitations such as expensiveness, proficient personnel, and complexity in procedures were noticed [29]. In addition to it, fiber-optic interferometer decorated with Fe3O4@SiO2@mesoSiO2 microspheres were successfully employed in the determination of GABA with advantages like high sensitivity and biocompatibility. But necessary modifications are still required in the further improvement of sensitivity parameters of the proposed sensor system [30]. In this study, we prepared an inexpensive and eco-friendly CCDs and then functionalized, or surface modified with APBA and NADP+ as CANs. Moreover, surface modification of CCDs allows achieving better sensitivity and selectivity towards the detection of GABA by the addition of GABase. Further, the fluorescence of CANs was effectively quenched by the addition of different concentrations of GABA in the presence of GABase.

3-Aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate 98% (APBA), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (98%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxy-succinimide (NHS), GABase from Pseudomonas fluorescens, 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl) piperazin-1-yl] ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer, Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+), Dopamine hydrochloride, l-glutamate, Epinephrine (adrenaline), Glycine, Serotonin (5-HT) and Human serum (male AB plasma) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ethanol (EtOH) and Methanol (MeOH) were purchased from Duksan pure chemicals (Ansan, South Korea). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with a pH of 7.4 (1× concentration) was directly used for measuring the pH of CCDs. Human CSF was obtained from LEE Biosolutions, USA. All experiments were conducted using deionized (DI) water and all chemicals were directly employed without any additional purification.

An optimally diluted solution of CCDs was used to record UV–visible spectra by employing a Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies of CCDs were investigated by employing Rigaku Ultima III X-ray diffractometer with Cu sealed tube (λ = 1.54178 Å) (40 kV, 30 mA, scan speed: 3°/min) in continuous scanning 2θ mode. All fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded using the Photon Technology International EasyLife II fluorometer system, which has auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 650 nm. The curve fitting data were obtained by using EasyLife II software through a serial RS232 and USB connection. The instrument response function was recorded using a 1% LUDOX® LS colloidal silica suspension in water as a background signal. The pH values of solutions were measured using the Thermo Scientific pH meter. The high-resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the CCDs was performed using a Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope. The zeta potential for the CCDs was captured by using an ELSZ-2000-series zeta potential and a particle size analyzer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for the CCDs and CANs were investigated using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha™ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer System equipped with 100–4000 eV range of motion, including a 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer and 128-channel detector equipped with an Al Kα micro-focused X-ray source. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature using a fluorescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, NJ, USA) with a xenon (Xe) lamp (Arc Lamp Housing, A-1010B™), monochromator, and power supply (Brytexbox). The excitation wavelength for fluorescence measurement was 353 nm.

Fresh corn seeds were ground into corn juice while adding water and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL). The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven and then allowed to cool to room temperature. A dark brown solution was obtained from this reaction. The supernatant CCD solution was centrifuged (at 10,000 rpm, 20 min) and dialyzed using a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 3.5 kDa) for approximately 24 h. Finally, powdered CDs were obtained, and the resulting CCDs were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

The CANs were prepared through a slight modification of the previous method [19]. In this method, CCDs and APBA combined with 100 mM EDC and 50 mM NHS. This mixture was magnetically stirred for 1 h and then incubated for approximately 12 h. The resulting CCD-APBA was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 5 min to remove any unreacted APBA. The CCD-APBA was then dispersed in 100 μL of 10 mM HEPES buffer and 0.5 mg NADP+ cofactor was added and incubated for approximately 10 h. The resulting CCD-APBA-NADP+ solution was purified via precipitation using methanol and dispersed in 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4). All spectra were recorded using 0.25 mg mL−1 of CCDs as a standard solution.

The quantum yield measurement was carried out for CCDs and CANs at an excitation wavelength of 353 nm by employing quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference using the Fluoro-Q2100 quantum yield system. Further, the quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation.
(1)
Quantum Yield = Ec /
La − Lc
here Ec denotes the emission produced by the direct excitation light, La is the total amount of excitation light, and Lc is the total amount of light measured after direct excitation [31].

The CANs have been employed for the detection of GABA in the presence of GABase via fluorescence sensing approach. In a typical run, 100 μL of CCDs and 100 μL of 5 U GABase in 5 mL of PBS buffer at pH 7.4 were added into a cuvette containing 700 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Soon after, the cuvette was placed in an ice chamber to maintain the reaction temperature below 5 °C. After that, different concentrations of GABA ranging from 0 to 90 μM were added into the above reaction mixture and incubated 15 to 20 min to allow for the proper interactions between reactants and substrates. Finally, corresponding all fluorescence spectra were recorded using photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometer at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of real samples and their preparation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the GABA, two real samples i.e. human CSF and serum were obtained from LEE Bio solutions, USA and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. Pretreatment of real samples was performed by following the previous literature [32,33]. The as-obtained samples were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm and the supernatants were collected. After the precipitation of the proteins in the samples using 0.5 mL acetonitrile, the resulting supernatants were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm and further employed for the fluorescence measurements.

The CCDs were prepared using corn juice as a precursor by employing hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for 8 h (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared CCDs show efficient blue color emission under ultraviolet (UV) illumination. The CCD solutions were separated via centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and resulting solutions were refrigerated for further use.

The size and morphology of the as-synthesized CCDs were analyzed by employing TEM and HRTEM. From the TEM results, CCDs revealed good uniformity and dispersion of CCDs with an average particle size of 3.8 nm (Fig. 1a, b, green circle). Further, HRTEM images of the CCDs demonstrated that maximum portion of particles are amorphous (red circle) and few are poor crystalline with lattice fringes (yellow circle) and lattice spacing of 0.218 nm (Fig. 1b, inset). Fig. 1c illustrates the particle size distribution of CCDs based on TEM images, which showed an average size of 2–6 nm. The XRD pattern of the CCDs shows a broad peak at (2θ = 20.72°), which is attributed to the graphitic structure (Fig. 1d) [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, XPS and Raman spectral studies of CCDs
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR studies were employed for the identification of functional groups on the CCDs and after the formation of CANs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The peaks identified at 1662, 1710, and 3317 cm−1 represent amide CO, COOH, OH, and NH stretching vibrations, respectively. This clearly demonstrates that the as-synthesized CCDs contain significant carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino functional groups. The other spectra were observed at 2925, 1519, and 1020 cm−1, corresponding to CH, CC, and CO stretching frequencies, respectively. The above peaks for the pure CCDs are illustrated in curve i in Fig. 2. The FTIR spectra of the CANs (curve ii) show a significant broadening of the OH stretching vibration at 3340 cm−1, which clearly indicates that CCDs were functionalized sufficiently by the APBA and NADP+ (curve ii, Fig. 2). The carboxyl functional groups observed in the FTIR spectra match the COH, CO, and OCO peaks obtained from the O 1s in the XPS analysis. Furthermore, the polar functional groups present in the as-synthesized CCDs provide superior aqueous solubility [36].

Further, elemental, surface compositional, and chemical state aspects of the CCDs were analyzed through XPS studies. The total survey spectrum from XPS revealed that the CCDs were composed of C, O, and N elements. Three peaks identified at 285, 531, and 400 eV can be attributed to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively (Fig. 3a). High-resolution XPS spectra deconvolutions of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s are presented in Fig. 3a–d. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s revealed three peaks at 284.98, 287.08, and 289.08 eV, which correspond to CC, CO, and COO groups, respectively (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s revealed three peaks at 531.58, 532.68, and 533.88 eV, which correspond to OCO, COH, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 3c) [37]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s revealed a peak at 400.08 eV, which indicates the presence of an NH group (Fig. 3d). The XPS data reveal several significant functional groups, including –COOH (acid) and –NH2 (amine), on the surface of the CCDs. Based on the presence of the above characteristic functional groups, the CCDs exhibited high stability and enough aqueous dispersion. The obtained XPS results strongly correlate to the results of FTIR in terms of the surface composition of elements. We analyzed the surface composition and chemical states of functionalized CCDs (CANs) by employing the XPS. The total survey spectrum obtained from XPS revealed that the CANs were consisting of C, O, N, B, P, and Na elements. The total six peaks were identified at 284.44, 531.33, 399.01, 191.59, 132.96, and 1071.69 eV, which can be attributed to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, B 1s, P 2p, and Na 1s spectra, respectively (Fig. S1a). The corresponding individual high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, B 1s, P 2p, and Na 1s are summarized in Fig. S1b–g. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, four peaks (Fig. S1b) were observed including the CB (283.8 eV), CC/CC (284.78 eV), CO/CN (286.28 eV), and OCO (287.78 eV). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum has shown three peaks (Fig. S1c) including OCO (531.08 eV), C-OH (531.98 eV), and CO (533.08 eV). As shown in Fig. S1d, N 1s spectrum has shown two characteristic peaks at 399.58 eV (NC) and 401.88 eV (NH). The B 1s spectrum (Fig. S1e) reveals the BC and BO groups at 188.48 eV and 191.58 eV, respectively [38]. From the XPS spectra of B 1s and N 1s, it is confirmed that the surface of the CCDs was modified with BC, BO and NH2 groups, respectively. Interestingly, P 2p and Na 1s peaks were observed in the high-resolution XPS spectra of CANs at 132.98 eV and 1071.58 eV, respectively, which indicates the functionalization of CCDs with NADP+ (Fig. S1f and g). These results suggest that CCDs were functionalized with APBA and NADP+. Moreover, Raman spectra of the CCDs revealed two characteristic bands of D and G at 1318 and 1587 cm−1, respectively. The D band at 1318 cm−1 corresponds to sp3 hybridization in carbon, whereas the G band at 1587 cm−1 corresponds to sp2 hybridized carbon, were presented in Fig. S2 in the supplementary information. The above results stated that the CCDs correspond to a graphitic structure of carbon dots [39,40]. The Zeta potential measurements were investigated to understand the surface charge of the CANs before and after the addition of the GABase enzyme. The zeta potential (ζ) of the CANs was found to be 5.11 mV, which indicates the presence of amine functional groups (positively charged functional groups) on the surface of CANs. After adding the GABase enzyme, the zeta potential (ζ) value of CANs was changed to −1.70 mV because of the negatively charged functional groups present in the GABase enzyme. The as-obtained zeta potential results are in good agreement with the FTIR experimental results, in which the broadening of the OH absorption peak is observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis, PL and time stability studies of CCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the CCDs were studied in deionized (DI) water. Fig. 4a represents the UV absorption, Photoluminescence (PL) excitation, and PL emission spectra, respectively. Curve I show two distinct transitions, namely π- π* and n- π*, which were observed at 225 and 282 nm, respectively. Curves II and III of the CCDs represent the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 353 and 446 nm, respectively. The inset (left, right) in Fig. 4a shows the images of CCDs under bright-field and UV-illumination, which demonstrates the strong blue emission. The PL intensity of the CCDs was tested in PBS buffer with a pH of 7.4 at different time intervals, where no fluctuations were observed in PL intensity up to 120 days. However, after 120 days, the PL intensity was slightly decreased (Fig. 4b). Further, excitation-dependent emission behaviors were exhibited by the CCDs and CANs, in which PL emission was red-shifted from 430 nm to 575 nm by changing the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 4c and d). The excitation-dependent emission behavior of CCDs may be attributed to the different functional groups existing on the surface of the CCDs [41]. On the other hand, to assess the fluorescence stability of the sensing system, CANs were mixed with GABase and observed under UV illumination for 1 h at regular intervals of 5 min and then fluorescence spectra were recorded as illustrated in (Fig. S3). However, no noticeable fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity were observed in CANs even after adding GABase. Further, in order to investigate the optical stability behaviors of the proposed sensing system towards the GABase, CANs were examined using different concentrations of GABA (10, 20, 30, 40, 70, 80, and 90 μM) with and without adding the GABase and further, corresponding UV–Vis spectra were recorded. The absorbance peaks in the CANs without adding GABase demonstrated maximum wavelength of 283 nm. However, after adding GABase, there was a slight shifting (blue shift) of peaks to a wavelength of 279 nm was noticed. The above results indicate that the sensing system is stable and not exhibited distinct changes even after adding GABase enzyme, as illustrated in (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence intensity of CCDs was not linearly affected by the concentrations of GABA from 0 to 90 μM. From Fig. S5, random behavior (fluorescence quenching and enhancing) in the fluorescence intensity of CCDs was observed by the addition of GABA. We believe this random behavior is mainly attributed to the zwitterionic nature of GABA. To compare the sensing performance of the as-functionalized CANs, we evaluated the fluorescence intensity dependence on GABA concentration (0–90 μM). Significant fluorescence quenching (almost 84%) was noticed in the CANs in presence of GABase with proper incubation time by the addition of GABA as illustrated in Fig. 5a. A linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and GABA concentration was observed with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9963 as shown in Fig. 5b. Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CANs at 446 nm in the absence and presence of analyte GABA, respectively. Furthermore, the LOD was calculated to be 6.46 μM based on 3σ/k, where σ and k represent the standard deviation and slope of the curve, respectively [42]. This shows better sensing capabilities of our system compared to other reported sensors for GABA (Table 1 ). The fluorescence quenching of CANs in the presence of GABase after the addition of GABA may be attributed to dynamic quenching or electron transfer process between enzyme and substrate. The above results clearly indicate that the developed enzymatic fluorescence sensing system is selective for the detection of GABA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity studies of CCDs and CANs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the CCDs was tested by varying the overall pH level of CCDs in DI water from 3 to 12, as illustrated in Fig. S6. The CCDs showed good stability in both acidic and basic conditions. Under extreme acidic conditions, approximately 40% fluorescence quenching was observed because of the electron transfer from the conduction bands of the CCDs to H+ ions. However, under basic conditions, negligible fluorescence quenching was identified, which may be due to the hydroxyl ions (OH−). At a neutral pH level, suitable for biological conditions, the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum value. The fluorescence stability was examined by using a PBS buffer (pH 7.4) at different concentrations (0–100 mmol L−1) of NaCl at intervals of 10 mmol L−1 to analyze the effect of ionic strength on the PL intensity of the CCDs. The results are presented in Fig. S7. The above results reveal the excellent fluorescence stability of CCDs under various physiological conditions, meaning the CCDs can be used in bio-sensing and bio-imaging. The above results provide a scope for the further applicability of CCDs, in bio-sensing and bio-imaging. Further, the selectivity of the CCDs, CANs, and after the addition of GABase was studied in the presence of significant neurotransmitters and amino acids, which are competitive interferants with the GABA (Fig. 6a). Significant fluorescence quenching of CANs was noticed in the presence of GABase, by the addition of GABA. However, no fluorescence quenching was noticed in the CANs, even after the addition of GABase in case of other interferants acetylcholine (ACh), glutamate (GLU), epinephrine (EP), serotonin (5-HT), aspartic acid (ASP), ascorbic acid (AA), and uric acid (UA). However, dopamine (DA) slightly quenched the fluorescence intensity of CANs, possibly due to the catechol groups present in DA. The Fig. 6b presents the images of CCDs alone, CANs, and after adding GABase with the above interferants, which are labeled in alphabetical order from (a to l). The significant PL quenching by GABA alone clearly demonstrates the excellent selectivity of CANs in the presence of GABase by comparing with other neurotransmitters and amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime decay studies and plausible PL quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL quenching mechanism of the CANs in the presence of the GABase enzyme by the addition of GABA was confirmed through time-resolved fluorescence lifetime decay studies, as shown in Fig. S8. The fluorescence decay curves can be fitted by using a double exponential function based on the following equation:
(2)
D
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
ai
exp
−
t τi
where, D, τi, and ai represent PL decay, lifetime, and corresponding pre-exponential factors, respectively [43]. The CANs showed a lifetime τ1 of 13.48 ns and lifetime τ2 of 0.032 ns. However, when GABA was added to the CANs in the presence of GABase, τ1 decreases to 10.45 ns and τ2 0.030 ns. Lifetime decay results also supported the above-mentioned mechanism, possibly via dynamic quenching or electron transfer between the enzyme and substrate. This plausible PL quenching mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis for GABA determination
ce:section.ce:para: The abnormal levels of GABA in CSF and serum are associated with the development of significant neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Picks disease as per the previous reports [44–46]. So, in order to evaluate the practicality and feasibility of the proposed sensing system, GABA was determined in the biological samples including human CSF and serum. The corresponding spiked samples were prepared using GABA at different concentrations (10, 30, 70, and 90 μM) in the real samples i.e. human CSF and serum. From Table 2 , the recovery values for CSF and serum were obtained in the range of 93.2–101.5% and 96.4–104.6%, respectively. Further, relative standard deviation (RSD) values of CSF and serum are within the range of 1.25–6.42% and 2.86–5.28%, respectively. The obtained RSD (from three replicates) value of less than 6.5% for the proposed sensing method is well supporting the practicality of the sensing system in the real samples.

In summary, we synthesized cost-effective and eco-friendly CDs from corn juice and functionalized with APBA and NADP+ (CANs). The as-prepared CANs along with the enzyme GABase allows highly selective detection of GABA at the micromolar level. The CCDs and CANs were demonstrated a quantum yield of 11% and 10.5%, respectively and further, CCDs have attained strong fluorescence intensity over a period of 180 days. This novel enzyme-based fluorescent sensor has achieved better sensitivity results for the detection of GABA at a linear range of 0–90 μM with a LOD of 6.46 μM. Moreover, the proposed enzymatic method presents better recovery results in real samples such as human CSF and serum. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report demonstrating the enzyme-based fluorescent sensing approach for the selective detection of neurotransmitter GABA using corn-derived CDs as a natural precursor. We believe that our experimental results would provide an excellent basis for detecting other neurotransmitters by applying enzymatic methods using natural carbon dots. Further, the proposed method can also be employed in the determination of GABA levels both in vitro and in vivo biomedical applications.
This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070).
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.05.320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: GABA detection with Nano-ITIES pipet electrode: a new mechanism, water/DCE−octanoic acid Interface

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.T. Iwai, M. Kramaric, D. Crabbe, Y. Wei, R. Chen, M. Shen, GABA Detection with Nano-ITIES Pipet Electrode: A New Mechanism, Water/DCE−Octanoic Acid Interface, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 3067-3072.
